commentary: Wrapped up the final low-tide survey for my thesis this summer. Eleven months of monthly intertidal sampling across eight Puget Sound sites, now officially in the analysis and writing phase. Huge thanks to the Coastal Ecology Lab field crew who showed up at 5am in the rain more times than I can count.
Spent the week learning more about community ordination in R than I thought possible. If anyone has a favorite resource for interpreting NMDS plots for intertidal community data, I'm all ears.

Our conservation team is hiring a Conservation Programs Associate to coordinate community science across the Salish Sea. If you care about ocean health and love working with volunteers, we'd love to hear from you.

Proud of the graduate students in the Coastal Ecology Lab who presented preliminary warming-survey results at our department seminar this week. The next generation of intertidal ecologists is in good hands.
New report out: nearshore water temperatures across the central Salish Sea ran above the 20-year average for the fifth consecutive summer. Full data and methods on our site.

Field season recap: three months, fourteen sites, and a lot of cold mornings. Grateful for the student researchers from PSIS who joined us this year. Long-term monitoring only works because people keep showing up.

Registration is open for our December Marine Biology seminar series. Graduate students will present thesis research spanning ocean warming, fisheries, and nearshore restoration. Free and open to the public.

Journal club this week: we dug into a great paper on thermal tolerance thresholds in intertidal invertebrates. The discussion about how lab-measured limits translate to field conditions was worth the price of admission.
We're hiring an Ecological Data Coordinator to help manage three decades of estuarine monitoring data. Strong R skills and a love of long-term datasets a plus. Applications open now.
Defended my qualifying exams today. To everyone in the Coastal Ecology Lab who quizzed me on benthic community dynamics until I dreamed about it, thank you. Onward to the dissertation.

Community science works. Our volunteer beach monitors logged over 4,000 observations this year, feeding directly into coastal management decisions across the state. Thank you to everyone who walked a beach for science.
Two years into the M.S. program and I still get excited every time the data tells me something I didn't expect. Today the salmon outmigration timing model finally cooperated.
Volunteers planted over 10,000 native Olympia oysters in South Puget Sound this season. Restoration is slow, hands-in-the-mud work, and it matters. More events coming this fall.
A reminder for graduate students: your methods section is a gift to your future self and to anyone trying to replicate your work. Write it like someone you respect will read it. Someone will.
New peer-reviewed study from our salmon team links nearshore habitat quality to juvenile survival in the Salish Sea. Open access link in comments.

Volunteered at a tide-pool education event this weekend and watched a six-year-old correctly identify an ochre sea star before half the adults could. Science communication starts early and it is the best part of this work.
Applications are open for our summer marine invertebrate zoology course. Six weeks in the San Juan Islands learning taxonomy, natural history, and field methods. Graduate credit available.
Spent the morning on the water collecting juvenile salmon samples. Some days the office is a research vessel in the rain and I wouldn't trade it. Field biology is a privilege.
Our Aquatic Resources team is recruiting for several positions supporting the management of Washington's state-owned aquatic lands. Help protect the nearshore for future generations.
Spent the week at the microscope keying out polychaetes. There is something deeply calming about invertebrate taxonomy, even when the literature contradicts itself three times in one genus.
Our new Pacific Northwest nearshore exhibit opens this month, featuring local intertidal species and the science behind protecting them. Come say hi to the sea stars.
Looking for a graduate student or recent grad with strong R skills for a part-time data role on our nearshore monitoring project. Salish Sea field experience a plus. Reach out if interested.
Abstract submissions are now open for the annual meeting. Sessions span community ecology, climate impacts, and ecological data science. Student travel awards available.
Inventory and gear maintenance day in the lab. A reminder that good field data starts with calibrated equipment and clean sample bottles. The unglamorous work is the foundation of everything.
